title: Chapter One: Seven Silhouettes
content: The sewing machine knows the pattern before I do.

It's 2 AM in my Brooklyn studio — a converted laundromat that still smells faintly of industrial starch and regret — and my fingers are moving on their own, feeding Victorian mourning crepe under the needle and into techwear mesh. The crepe is the real thing: matte, crinkled, the kind of fabric they stopped making when they stopped knowing how to grieve properly. I sourced it from an estate sale in Greenwich six years ago, paid in cash, and the widow who sold it to me didn't know what she had. They never do.

The machine hums against my fingertips, that familiar vibration traveling up through the presser foot and into the pads of my fingers, and my hands just — go. Lockstitch. Backstitch. Lockstitch. The rhythm of it is older than this city, older than the language I'm thinking in. My fingers have known this stitch pattern since before Manhattan had a skyline. The muscle memory of four hundred years is a quiet tyranny. My body does the work and my mind drifts somewhere else, and that's how I like it, and that's the problem, because—
I stop.
The needle hovers, mid-descent. The dress hangs half-formed on the dummy by the window, and even in the amber wash of the streetlight coming through the glass, I can see it. The silhouette.

Nipped waist. Flowing through the hip. A single architectural seam running from the left shoulder down to the hem, like a fault line. The same shape I've cut seven times now. Seven centuries. Different fabrics, different names, different faces on the women — and the men, and the ones who were both and neither — who wore them. But the silhouette. The silhouette is always the same.
"Not again."

My voice comes out hoarse. I haven't spoken to another human in nine hours. I have, however, had a full conversation with the sewing machine about tension settings, so I'm not entirely without social contact.

I reach past the machine for the cold pizza on the windowsill — three hours old, Canadian bacon because the place on Myrtle only does delivery after midnight if you order the specialty, and the specialty is Canadian bacon. I bite in. The cheese has congealed into something with the structural integrity of linoleum, and the sauce has gone sweet and sharp in a way that makes my molars ache. I chew anyway. I've eaten worse. I've eaten far worse. Dunkerque, 1662, was worse. This is fine.
I stare at the dress. The dress stares back.

I should take it apart. I should grab the seam ripper and undo every stitch and fold the crepe back into its archival box and pretend my hands didn't just make the same shape they've been making since the reign of James-fucking-I. But it's 2 AM, and the dress is beautiful, and I'm so tired of fighting the things my body already knows.
So I leave it on the dummy and crawl onto the mattress on the floor and close my eyes.
The radiator screams me awake at seven.

It's a 1952 monster, bolted to the wall like a tumor, and every morning at 7:00 sharp it announces itself with a clanging that sounds like someone dropping silverware down a metal staircase. Clang. Clang. Clang-clang. The super says he'll fix it. The super has been saying that since the Biden administration.

I sit up. The dress is still on the dummy. It looks better in daylight, which means it looks worse for me — the crepe catches the weak morning sun and looks almost alive, almost like skin, and the architectural seam catches the light and throws it back in a way that shouldn't work but does. I designed this dress in 1623. I didn't know it was a design then. I just drew a shape that wouldn't leave my head, and I cut it out of wool broadcloth, and the woman who wore it told me I'd made her feel like architecture, and I didn't know what she meant but I've spent four centuries finding out.
My phone buzzes. FaceTime. Suki.
I answer with the camera pointing at my ceiling because I look like something a Victorian child would see in a fever dream.
"Wow," Suki says. "You look great. Very corpse-chic."
"The radiant glow of four hours sleep. What do you want?"
"Babe." I can hear her chewing gum — that aggressive, rhythmic snap that means she's caffeinated and on a mission. "The Ashford Foundation gig tonight. They need extra hands. Three hundred an hour."
I close my eyes. The Ashford Foundation. The name sits in my mouth like a coin I can't identify.
"I'd rather sew my fingers together."

"I'll take that as a yes." Snap. Snap. "Orlando. Babe. Three hundred an hour. That's rent. That's — that's the Vintage Singer repair you've been putting off. That's not eating pizza off the windowsill for a week."
"It's not the windowsill's fault. It's a very convenient windowsill."
"Seven PM. The Ashford estate on Fifth. Wear black. Don't be late. Don't be ornery."
"I'm always ornery."
"I know. That's why I said don't be." And she's gone.

I look at the schedule on my wall — the one I wrote in Sharpie on a piece of butcher paper because I ran out of real planner pages in March. Morning: alterations shop, Midtown, nine to one. Afternoon: private fitting with the client who's rescheduled four times now, each reschedule delivered via a text that begins with "So sorry, something came up—" and ends with at least three emojis. Evening: the gala. Three jobs. Three different versions of invisible. This is what immortality looks like in 2026: hustling for rent in a city that would literally eat you if you stopped moving.

The alterations shop sits between a bodega and a shuttered Foot Locker on 38th Street, and the fluorescent lighting does unspeakable things to everyone's complexion. I'm behind the counter when the client arrives — a woman in a St. John knit she paid too much for, with a hem that's running like a scared animal.
"This is completely wrong," she says, holding up the skirt. "I told them tea length. This is practically a maxi."

She didn't tell them tea length. I know this because I altered the original work order when she wasn't looking. She said "just above the ankle" and then changed her mind on the way out, which she does every single time, because she doesn't actually know what she wants. What she wants is to feel like she made a decision and someone listened, and that's a garment problem the way a hangnail is a cardiovascular problem — technically connected, mostly irrelevant.

What she should be wearing is an A-line in a stable weave that doesn't bias-cut on her. The St. John is fighting her hips instead of flowing past them. The shoulder seams are a quarter-inch off on each side, which means she bought off the rack and didn't bother tailoring, which means she thinks fitting is optional, which means she's never stood in front of a mirror and felt a garment line up with her skeleton like it was made for her — like it was the architectural expression of her own bones. She hasn't earned that. She doesn't even know it exists.

But I smile. I pin. I mark the new hem with silk thread — which she didn't pay for and won't notice — and I finish the alteration in thirty seconds flat. Thirty seconds. My needle moves in a way that should make her stop and stare, should make her ask how a human hand can feed fabric that fast with stitch spacing that precise, but she's checking her phone, so she doesn't notice the miracle.
No one ever does.
"Better?" I ask.
"Much. Thank you." She doesn't look up. She's already gone, mentally, to whatever comes next.
I turn and catch my reflection in the shop mirror — the one with the crack running through the lower left quadrant, the one the owner won't replace because this is Midtown and mirrors cost rent —
The face looking back isn't mine.

For half a second, it's a young man. Longer hair, dark, pushed behind an ear that's slightly too big for his face. Startled eyes. Mouth open like he's about to say something, like he's about to call out to me from across a room, from across a century. He looks terrified. He looks like someone who just realized the shape of his own life.
I look away.

The fluorescent lights hum. The fitting room curtain sways from a draft that shouldn't exist. I focus on the St. John knit and the silk thread and the absolute, mathematical certainty of a well-placed hem, and I do not look at the mirror again.
Between the afternoon fitting (rescheduled again, obviously, this time with a balloon emoji) and the gala shift, I go home.

I kneel beside the bed and pull out the locked drawer. The lock is a brass number, 1920s, that I bought from a locksmith in Prague the last time I was someone else. The key lives on a chain around my neck, under my shirt, against my sternum. I don't know why I still wear it there. Habit. Superstition. The desire to feel something metal and fixed against a body that isn't.
The sketchbook is inside.

It's not one sketchbook, obviously. It's dozens, rebound into a single object that's thicker than a phone book and twice as heavy. I've carried it across oceans, through fires, through one very memorable flood in Venice where I lost everything except this and the clothes on my back. The pages are different textures, different weights. The earliest are vellum — real vellum, scraped and prepared by my own hands. Then laid paper. Then machine-made. Then the slick coated stock of the twentieth century.
I flip.
1623. Ink wash. A woman standing in a doorway, the light behind her so she's all silhouette. The strokes are uncertain, searching. I didn't know how to draw yet. I just knew the shape.

1702. Charcoal. The same figure, but now they're turning, and there's a fluidity to the line that wasn't there before. I was getting better. I was learning that the shape had a direction, a turning point, a moment of becoming.

1811. Architectural draftsmanship. Grid lines. Measurements. I'd gotten precise out of self-defense, because precision was the only thing the Royal Academy respected, and I was a man then, or close enough to pass, and men were expected to be precise about the things they loved.

2024. Digital roughs, printed and glued in. The same shape, but now rendered in Procreate, now built from layers, now a thing that could be shared and duplicated and made into something that this century calls "content."

Every page. The same silhouette. The nipped waist, the flowing hip, the seam like a fault line. I've been drawing this shape for four hundred and three years. I've never figured out what it is. I've never stopped.

I flip to the last page. It's blank — I keep the last page blank, have always kept the last page blank, because the shape isn't finished, because it's never finished, because I'm still drawing it, still chasing it, still waking at two in the morning with my hands moving on their own and my machine humming under my fingers and a dress on the dummy that I designed before this city had a name.

I close the sketchbook. I lock the drawer. I pull on the catering uniform — black trousers, black shirt, black apron. The uniform of the invisible. The costume of someone who serves but is not seen. I've worn worse disguises. I've been worse things.

The walk to the Ashford estate takes me along Fifth Avenue, past the kind of stores that have doormen and display windows lit like altars. It's cold — February in New York, the kind of cold that isn't dramatic, just relentless — and I'm walking fast because the uniform isn't warm and because walking fast means thinking less.
But I stop.

A storefront on the corner. One of those "accessible luxury" brands — the kind that takes runway concepts and strips them of everything interesting until they're just shapes for purchase. And in the window, on a mannequin: a dress.
My dress.

Not my dress. Not really. A clumsy approximation. The silhouette is there — nipped waist, flowing hip — but the seam is wrong, centered instead of offset, and the fabric is some poly-blend that photographs well but drapes like a shower curtain. They've taken something that took me four centuries to understand and reduced it to a SKU number. They've taken a shape that lives in my fingers and my blood and my muscle memory and made it cheap and fast and wrong.

The streetlight catches the mannequin and makes it glow — plastic skin turned spectral, a ghost wearing my silhouette like a costume. The display is garish and imprecise, and it should be funny, and it isn't. It's just sad. It's just another century that doesn't recognize what it's looking at.
But that's not what stops me.

Behind the mannequin, in a small framed photograph: a man at last year's Met Gala. Rowan Ashworth. He's wearing a velvet jacket and looking slightly to the left of the camera, like he's watching something the photographer can't see. He has the kind of face that doesn't need to be handsome — angular, interested, alive. Dark hair pushed back off his forehead. A slight smile that isn't quite one.
And on his right hand: an emerald ring.
My chest constricts.

It's not a heart thing. It's not a breath thing. It's something lower, deeper — something in the architecture of my ribs, in the space between my spine and my sternum. A recognition. A lock turning in a door I didn't know was there.

The smell of the mourning crepe rises unbidden — that dry, herbal must of fabric preserved from an era that dressed its grief in texture. I can feel it in my nose, in the back of my throat, and suddenly I'm not on Fifth Avenue anymore, I'm somewhere else, some when else, standing in a doorway with light behind me and a shape in my hands that I don't understand yet, and there's someone on the other side of the threshold, someone reaching—
I don't know this man. I've never met him. He's a name on a foundation, a face in a photograph, a stranger in a window.
But my body — this body, the one I've worn for nine years, the one that's been quiet for nine years, the longest silence in centuries — my body knows something my mind has buried.
The hum starts under my skin.

It's a frequency I haven't felt since 2017, since the last time I shifted, since the last time my bones reorganized themselves around a new arrangement of self. It starts in my sternum and radiates outward, through my shoulders, down my arms, into the tips of my fingers — the same fingers that know the stitch pattern, the same fingers that have drawn the same silhouette for four hundred years. The hum is the sound of my body about to do something. The hum is the sound of change. The hum is the sound of the lock and the key and the door between what I remember and what I've made myself forget.
I look at the photograph. Rowan Ashworth looks slightly to the left of the camera, at something I can't see.
I keep walking.

The gala is in twenty minutes. My hands are shaking. The hum persists, a sound only I can hear, a vibration only I can feel, and it's saying something in a language my mind has forgotten but my body still speaks.

I keep walking. I don't know why a stranger's face is making my body remember. I don't know why the emerald ring feels like a word I've been trying to say for four hundred years. I don't know what's buried under the silence of these nine years, and I'm not sure I want to find out.
But the hum knows. The hum has always known.
And it's getting louder.
